Big homes with yards are ideal but not required, as long as these dogs get exercise and plenty of love and attention. Boxadors are protective of their loved ones and friendly with people, children, and other dogs. Don’t leave them alone for long periods, though, or else they may become bored and destructive. DogTime recommends  this big, spacious crate  to give your big Boxador a place to rest and relax. You should also  pick up this dog brush and massager  for your short-haired pup! See below for all Boxador facts and mixed dog breed characteristics!

The Boxer is a medium-to-large-sized Mastiff-type breed that originated in Germany in the 19th century. Historians believe that this breed descended from the now-extinct Bullenbeisser, which hunters used for hunting large game like boar and deer. They developed the modern Boxer by crossing the Bullenbeisser with various other breeds, including the  English Bulldog  and the  Mastiff . The George Clooney of the dog world, Boxers are lookers with a sense of humor and an underlying sweetness. The Boxer is known for their muscular and athletic build. With a distinctive square-shaped head, strong jaws, and a short, shiny coat, Boxers exude an air of alertness and strength. Highly regarded for their intelligence, loyalty, and playful nature, Boxers make excellent companions as family pets and working dogs. Despite their strong and athletic appearance, they are gentle and patient with children. Boxers also earn a reputation for being protective of their loved ones, making them excellent watchdogs. When considering a Boxer, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you’re on the search for Boxer puppies, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Boxer breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Boxerdoodle, typically a medium-sized dog breed, is a mix between the  Boxer  and  Poodle . Friendly, smart, and playful, these pups inherited some of the best qualities from both of their parents. Boxerdoodles are also sometimes known as  Boxerpoos . Like other Poodle mixes, you can stil find these designer “doodle” dogs in shelters and breed specific rescues, so remember to always adopt! Don’t shop if you’re looking to add one of these dogs to your home! The Boxerdoodle makes for an exceptionally playful and super friendly dog who can become a great addition to a family. They are active dogs who aren’t well suited to apartment living because of their innate need for exercise and play. If you have a fenced-in yard, your kids and the dog will become best play buddies; although, it’s always important to supervise sessions involving small children. As a heads up, due to the range of size between different types of Poodle, your Boxerdoodle could end up being anywhere from small to large in size. When considering a Boxerdoodle, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you’re on the search for Boxerdoodle puppies and decide to purchase, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Boxerdoodle breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Boxmatian is a mixed breed dog — a cross between the  Boxer  and  Dalmatian  dog breeds. Playful, goofy, and protective, these pups inherited some of the best qualities from both of their parents. You can find these mixed breed dogs in shelters and breed specific rescues, so remember to always adopt! Don’t shop if you’re looking to add a Boxmatian to your home! The Boxmatian is a bit like a cartoon dog come to life! They have a  larger-than-life personality  and love to play and goof around. If you have one of these dogs in your home, you’ll be laughing a lot. Properly trained, the mixed breed is affectionate and loyal to their humans and will take on a guardian role. But be warned! Correct training from the outset is essential to offset any aggressive tendencies and destructive behavior. For these reasons, this may not be the best dog to bring into a family with small children. Boxmatians also require a lot of exercise and are not apartment dogs on any level. See below for all mixed dog breed traits and facts about Boxmatians!

The Boxweiler is a large crossbreed between the  Boxer  and  Rottweiler . Loving, loyal, and bright, these pups inherited some of their parents’ best qualities. Boxweilers go by several names, including Box Rotty, Boxer Rottie, and Boxie Rottie. d. Because of their parentage, the Boxweiler temperament is affectionate, energetic, curious, and loyal. Due to their high energy levels, it’s best if they’re in a larger home with a yard rather than an apartment. They can also easily get bored and require adequate mental stimulation for their intelligent minds. They’re very loyal dogs who get along well with any family size. They also make excellent guard dogs without being overly aggressive. If you want an energetic dog who will keep you on your toes, alert you to potential dangers, and love you unconditionally, then the Boxweiler may be perfect for you! When considering a Boxweiler, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you’re searching for Boxweiler puppies, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Boxweiler breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Height: 14 to 18 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: Boykin Spaniels are friendly, confident, and adaptable dogs that make great companions for families and individuals. They are affectionate and loyal, and they love being around people. They are also patient and  gentle with children , and they typically get along well with other dogs. While prized for their highly skilled  hunting skills , these friendly dogs are loved for their charm, personality, and adaptability. Their affectionate and loyal disposition makes them devoted family pets, forming strong bonds with their human family members. Boykin Spaniels also thrive on human companionship and enjoy being an active part of the family’s daily activities. Patient and gentle, Boykin Spaniels make excellent playmates and companions for kids of all ages. Additionally, these easy-going pups typically get well with other dogs in the household. Their social nature and willingness to interact with others make them a delightful addition to multi-pet households. As always, early  socialization  is key. When considering a Boykin Spaniel, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you’re on the search for  Boykin Spaniel puppies  and decide to purchase, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Boykin Spaniel breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Bracco Italiano is a large-sized Sporting dog from Italy and one of the oldest pointing breeds. Tough and reliable, the Bracco Italiano has been a beloved hunting  dog breed  in their native Italy for thousands of years but only joined the American Kennel Club (AKC) in 2022. In recent years, the Italian Pointer gained notice as a  family companion  with a sweet and affectionate personality. Their long ears, droopy lips, soulful expression, and lean build give the gentle Bracco Italiano a distinctive and rugged look. Believed to be an ancient breed, this Italian favorite dates back to the fourth or fifth century B.C. The Bracco Italiano goes by several other names, including the Italian Pointer, The Italian Pointing Dog, and Bracco. The Bracco Italiano temperament is playful, determined, loyal, devoted, and stubborn. They are also highly intelligent and have endless amounts of energy, which is probably worth considering for any  apartment-dwelling  pet parents. The Bracco is also  well suited for all household  sizes, from single individuals to large families with children. Though they are not watchdog material, they will let you know if they sense a change in their environment. When considering an Italian Pointer, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you’re looking for Bracco Italiano or Bracci Italiani (plural) puppies and decide to purchase one, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Bracco Italiano breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Braque du Bourbonnais is a medium-sized, ancient purebred pointing dog originally from France. Affectionate, calm, and intelligent, these pups have some of the best qualities of any dog breed out there today. This breed goes by several other names, including Bourbonnais Pointer, Bourbonnais Pointing Dog, French Pointing Dog, French Pointer, Braques Francaises, and Braque Bourbonnais. These affectionate pups thrive in homes with yards and may not find happiness with apartment living. You may want to keep this in mind if you’re a city-dwelling pet parent thinking of adding a Braque du Bourbonnais puppy to your home. Highly social, these people-oriented need to be around their humans. They are well-suited for families or large households with more than one person at home. If you want a gentle and energetic pup, the Braque du Bourbonnais may be the right dog for you! When considering a Braque du Bourbonnais, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Braque du Bourbonnais breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Height: 22 to 27 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The Briard, also known as the Berger de Brie, is a medium-to-large-sized herding breed originally from France. The Briard is perhaps most easily recognized by their shaggy coat which ranges in color from solid black, fawn, or gray to various shades of tawny. Beyond their unique appearance, the breed is renowned for their intelligence and loyalty. They excel in various roles from herding livestock to serving as a faithful family companion and guardian. Their gentle yet protective nature makes them excellent with children and a natural watchdog. With their agility and trainability, Briards have also found success in dog sports and obedience competitions. First bred as herding and guard dogs to protect flocks, these canines adapted to a variety of working roles during World War I. They served troops as sentries, ammunition carriers, messengers, and medic dogs. Today, the Briard dog typically enjoys the life of a companion dog. However, they still show their versatility and working nature with their great successes in obedience, agility, conformation, herding, carting, and tracking competitions. When considering a Briard, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase a Briard puppy, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Briard breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Height: 17 inches to 20 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The Brittany, formerly known as Brittany Spaniel, is a compact yet energetic  sporting dog breed , Originating from the Brittany region of northwestern France, this well-muscled gundog is a favorite of hunters for its endurance and versatility in the field. With a dense, flat or wavy coat and floppy ears, the Brittany combines an athletic build with a cheerful, eager-to-please temperament, making an excellent hunting partner and an affectionate family dog for active households who love the outdoors. Highly intelligent and easy to train, the Brittany thrives with regular exercise and activity. It excels in sports like agility, obedience, and competitive retrieving events in addition to its traditional roles in upland hunting and retrieving waterfowl. When considering a Brittany, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase a Brittany Spaniel puppy, it’s crucial to choose  a reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs.

The Broholmer, also called the Danish Mastiff, is a large working dog from Denmark originally used for stag hunting. Despite their imposing appearance as a Mastiff-type breed, the Broholmer’s temperament is known to be calm, protective, and friendly. Powerfully built with a strong, muscled body and a large head, the Broholmer stands 27 to 30 inches tall at the shoulder and can easily weigh up to 150 pounds. Although first bred as a hunter, the Broholmer later became guardians of homes and farms. While relatively rare outside of Denmark, the Broholmer has increased in popularity in the United States. These protective dogs can do fine in apartments but, because of their large size, are better suited for houses, preferably with a yard. This might be worth considering for any city-dwelling pet parents considering adding a Broholmer puppy to their home. As long as you can give these pups enough  exercise and mental stimulation , they should do just fine adapting to apartment life. Additionally, they’re social pack dogs and like to be around people, so larger households or families with children are perfect for them. When considering a Broholmer, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you’re searching for  Broholmer puppies  and decide to purchase one, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Broholmer breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Height: 7 to 8 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The Brussels Griffon is a small and charming dog breed that originated in Belgium. They were originally bred as  rat catchers  in stables, but their adorable looks and delightful personality quickly endeared them to people as companion dogs. You can easily recognize a Brussels Griffon for their adorable, furry gargoyle appearance, which includes a short, pushed-in face with large, expressive eyes. They have a wiry and dense coat that can come in different colors, such as red, black, black and tan, or belge (a mix of black and reddish-brown). Their ears are may be cropped to stand upright, enhancing their alert and attentive expression. This breed is highly affectionate and forms strong bonds with their owners. They thrive on human companionship and are always eager to be by their side. Their small size makes them well-suited for  apartment living,  and they are equally content living in a house with a yard. Brussels Griffons are intelligent and enjoy mental stimulation. They are quick learners which can come in handy when raising a  Brussel Griffons puppy ! However, they can also be a bit stubborn at times, so a consistent, kind, and patient hand is essential. Being lively and curious dogs, they love playtime and will happily join in on various activities. Despite their playful nature, they are not overly energetic and are quite content with moderate exercise, such as short walks and indoor games.  Socialization  is crucial for Brussels Griffons to develop well-rounded and confident personalities. Introducing them to different people, places, and experiences from an early age helps prevent any potential shyness or fearfulness.

The Bugg, a cross between the  Boston Terrier  and  Pug , is an intelligent, loving, and spunky companion dog. The Bugg’s combination of unique Pug and Boston Terrier traits results in a dog with a comical, clownish appearance and an entertaining, affectionate personality. They are loyal and devoted to their families, and are also known for their playful, energetic, and sometimes mischievous natures. While intelligent, Buggs can also be quite stubborn, requiring  positive reinforcement  during training. Overall, their gentle, lovable, and charming personalities makes them excellent companions in the right home. These cute pups are great apartment and house dogs, alike. Since they don’t require as much  exercise  as larger breeds, they’re an excellent choice for seniors or first-time owners. They love being the center of attention, and if you are looking for a sweet dog that’ll stay by your side, then the Bugg is a perfect choice. When considering  a Bugg puppy , it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase, it’s crucial to choose  a reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Bugg puppy breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Bull Arab is a hybrid breed dog with ancestry linked to the English Bull Terrier,  Greyhound , shorthair Pointers, and later, larger breeds like the Mastiff and  Great Dane . Strong, loyal, and active, the Bull Arab inherited some of the best qualities from their lineage. Bull Arabs go by several names, including the Australian Pig Dog and Aussie Pig. You may find these dogs in shelters and breed specific rescues, so remember to  adopt ! Don’t shop! These independent and energetic dogs often work as guard and hunting dogs in their native Australia. The  Bull Arab  was bred specifically to have the hunting and scent-tracking skills of their ancestors. When larger breeds like Mastiffs and Great Danes made their way into the bloodline, they became excellent guard dogs, too. Even though Bull Arabs are loyal to their humans, this large and sometimes intimidating dog may not be the best option for first-time adopters. This breed requires heavy socialization and training, or they can become aggressive. If you are looking for a dog who doubles as a big snuggle buddy and a guard, and you feel up to the task of consistent training, the Bull Arab might be the right dog for you! See below for all dog breed traits and facts about Bull Arabs!

The Bull-Pei is a mixed breed dog–a cross between the  Chinese Shar-Pei  and  English Bulldog  breeds. Medium in size, loving, and loyal, these pups inherited some of the best traits from both of their parents. Despite their unfortunate status as a designer breed, you may find these pups in shelters and breed specific rescues, so remember to adopt! Don’t shop! Bull-Peis make excellent companions and guard dogs without being too large or requiring much exercise. They’re striking to look at because of their wrinkles. Just make sure that you have the time to keep your pup clean and dry, as they are prone to getting skin infections between the wrinkles. These dogs can live in apartments or houses. If you’re looking for a chill companion dog who looks quite distinguished, the Bull-Pei may be perfect for you. DogTime recommends  this carrier for traveling  with your small Bull-Pei. You should also  pick up this dog brush and massager  for your short-haired pup! See below for all mixed dog breed traits and facts about Bull-Peis!

Height: 21 to 22 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The English Bull Terrier, also called the Bull Terrier, is a medium-sized terrier breed from the United Kingdom known for their unique “egg-shaped” head and glossy coat. Perhaps one of the most iconic-looking dog breeds, they can immediately be recognized for their muscular build and sloped foreheads, a hallmark of their appearance. To the outside eye, these Pit Bull dogs also can have a somewhat imposing appearance, but these pups are just as goofy and comical as can be. The English Bull Terrier temperament is friendly, affectionate, and outgoing. Their playful, happy-go-lucky attitude makes them great companions for families. However, early socialization and consistent training are essential because they can be strong-willed and mischievous. They also have a lot of energy and enjoy outdoor activities and exercise. As a result, they may be more compatible with active individuals or families. They are also incredibly loyal and protective, making them excellent watchdogs. It’s always advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, choosing a reputable breeder is crucial if you’re searching for Bull Terrier puppies. Additionally, it’s important to conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs.  Reputable breeders  prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs. They also conduct necessary health screenings and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Bullador is a mixed breed dog–a cross between the  Labrador  and  English Bulldog  breeds. These pups fall into the medium-to-large size range. Active and loyal, Bulladors inherited some of the best traits from both of their parents. Bulladors should not be confused with  Labrabulls –Labrador Retriever and Pit Bull mixes. Despite their unfortunate status as a designer breed, you can find these mixed pups in shelters and breed specific rescues, so remember to adopt! Don’t shop! If you are a longtime dog parent and want to add a social, furry companion to your family, please step right up! Big homes with yards are ideal but not required. Bulladors can live in apartments as long as they get plenty of exercise and lots of love. They’re protective of their loved ones and friendly with people, children, and other dogs. Don’t leave them alone for long periods, though, or else they may become bored and destructive. DogTime recommends  this dog bed to give a good night’s sleep  to your medium-sized Bullador. You should also  pick up this dog brush and massager  for your short-haired pup! See below for all Bullador facts and mixed dog breed characteristics!

The Bullboxer Pit is a mixed breed dog — a cross between the  Boxer  and  American Pit Bull Terrier  dog breeds. Powerful, faithful, and energetic, these pups inherited some of the best traits from both of their parents. Bullboxer Pits are also sometimes called the  Pixoter  or American Bullboxer. You may find these mixed breed dogs in shelters and rescues, so remember to always adopt! Don’t shop if you’re looking to add one of these pups to your home! Bullboxer Pits can be loyal and protective companions, and they often bond well with children. But due to the dog’s natural strength and sometimes stubborn streak, it is imperative that they are socialized both from a very young age and throughout their lifetime. Reward-based training works particularly well with this mixed breed. An attentive and responsible pet parent will bring the best out of the Bullboxer Pit. See below for all mixed dog breed traits and facts about Bullboxer Pits!

Height: 12 to 15 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The English Bulldog, also called the Bulldog or British Bulldog, is a medium-sized Mastiff-type dog breed in the non-sporting group known for their distinctive appearance and friendly disposition. Dating back to the 13th century, this breed has evolved from their bull-baiting past to become gentle and affectionate companions. They can easily be recognized for their unique features, including a wrinkled face and pushed-in nose, muscular build, and compact body. As a breed standard, Bulldogs stand 12 to 15 inches tall at the shoulder and weigh up to 50 pounds. Females are slightly smaller, usually only weighing up to 40 pounds. With their friendly temperament, the English Bulldog forms strong bonds with their family, making them an ideal family pet. Along with their courage and tenacity, they retain a fearless spirit and protective instincts, making them excellent watchdogs. Despite their muscular, sometimes imposing build, Bulldogs are surprisingly gentle and docile, making them great companions for children and families. Because of their calm and easy-going temperament, Bulldogs adapt well to apartment living. They are content with a stroll or playtime in the backyard. When considering a Bulldog, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase a Bulldog puppy, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Bulldog breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Bullmastiff is a large, muscular dog bred in England in the late 1800s as a guard dog to protect gamekeepers and their property from poachers. Bullmastiffs have a gentle and loyal nature, however, they can also be very protective of their family and home. A natural protector, the Bullmastiff has an innate sense of responsibility towards its family and property. Although they have an imposing size, Bullmastiffs make outstanding companions for families with children. In fact, their gentle and patient nature makes them well-suited for this role. Additionally, they are very protective of their family and home, which can be reassuring for parents. Finally, they are relatively low-maintenance dogs, which makes them a good choice for busy families. Training this breed is a gratifying experience, as they exhibit a willingness to learn and please their owners. Due to their strong protective instincts, early  socialization  and consistent  training  are essential to ensure a well-rounded and well-behaved Bullmastiff. In   addition, this breed’s loyal and devoted nature, combined with an innate sense of judgment, makes them excellent watchdogs, as they instinctively detect potential threats and therefore react accordingly. In terms of exercise, the Bullmastiff prefers moderate physical activity. Regular walks, play sessions, and mental stimulation are enough to keep them content and in good health. They can easily  adapt  well to urban living, provided they receive attention and exercise. This giant guardian is a breed that embodies strength, loyalty, and intelligence. Their vigilant nature, combined with a gentle and affectionate demeanor towards their loved ones, makes them a cherished addition to any family.

The Bullmatian is a mixed breed dog — a cross between the  Bulldog  and  Dalmatian  dog breeds. Loving, energetic, and friendly, these pups inherited some of the best qualities from both of their parents. You may find these mixed breed dogs in shelters and breed specific rescues, so remember to always adopt! Don’t shop if you’re looking to add a Bullmatian to your home! Bullmatians are top-notch companion dogs. This mixed breed is known to be loving and friendly and also does very well around children. However, this energetic dog will need a high amount of regular exercise and a variety of play sessions. If you’re a rookie dog owner, then you should know that Bullmatians have a reputation for stubborness at times — they’re most likely to make a good fit for a seasoned dog owner who has a lot of experience training dogs. See below for all Bullmatian facts and mixed dog breed characteristics!

Height: 9 to 10 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The Cairn Terrier is a small working terrier developed on the Isle of Skye in Scotland. If you’re a fan of The Wizard of Oz, you already know the breed. Farmers used them to rid their property of vermin, and they needed a dog with courage, tenacity, and intelligence — characteristics still found in today’s Cairn. Cairn Terriers are sensible, independent, and friendly dogs who excel as family companions. Compact and affectionate, these dogs do well in apartments and large homes with novice pet parents and experienced dog lovers alike. However, they have high energy and will need their humans to keep them active with walks and play sessions. This dog breed doesn’t enjoy being left home alone for long hours during the day, so make sure you can provide them with plenty of love and attention for their well-being if you decide that this is the breed for you. When considering a Cairn Terrier, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase a Cairn Terrier puppy, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Cairn Terrier breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Height: 19 to 24 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The Canaan Dog, a pariah  breed , boasts an ancient lineage that has thrived in Israel’s desert for millennia. Many believe it’s the same breed that Hebrews used in biblical times to herd and safeguard their flocks and settlements, a role it continues to fulfill among  Bedouins  and  Druse  communities today. In Europe and North America, people cherish Canaan Dogs as companions and enthusiastic participants in dog sports like conformation,  agility , and obedience. These dogs are remarkably adaptable, capable of thriving in apartment living as long as their  exercise  needs are met. However, given their historical background as pack animals, they require consistent training and firm leadership. Though highly intelligent, they can display occasional stubbornness, making  positive reinforcement  a key to unlocking their eagerness to please. With the right approach, you’ll have a devoted  pup  that embraces modern life while honoring its ancient heritage. When considering a Canaan Dog, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase a  Canaan Dog puppy , it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Canaan Dog dog breeders prioritize health and temperament, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Height: 23 inches to 27 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The Cane Corso, also known as the Italian Mastiff, is a large working dog  originally bred as guardians of farms and livestock. These powerful dogs possess a loyal and protective nature. However, their imposing stature and past association with dog fighting have unfortunately led to misconceptions about their temperament. In reality, a Cane Corso dog can form strong bonds with their families. While they may be reserved towards strangers, proper socialization from a young age helps them become well-adjusted members of the family. A Cane Corso’ short, dense coat requires minimal grooming but sheds moderately, with heavier shedding twice a year. The breed standard allows for black, fawn, brindle (any of these colors with brindle markings), and red. Some may have white markings on the chest, toes, and muzzle. The Cane Corso boasts a well-muscled, athletic physique and their size can be imposing and intimidating at first glance. The Cane Corso’s story started thousands of years ago. Their lineage traces back to the Molossers, giant Mastiff-type guard dogs bred by the ancient the Greek people, the Molossi. Fast forward to the Roman conquest of Greece, and these impressive dogs migrated to Italy. There, the Romans interbred them with local breeds, creating the ancestors of both the Cane Corso and the Neapolitan Mastiff. Believe it or not, Cane Corsi were some of the first military service dogs! The Romans called them “pireferi” and tasked them with carrying flaming buckets of oil strapped to their backs – a fiery tactic to scare and disorient enemy soldiers. After the Roman Empire fell in the 5th century, the Cane Corso’s duties became a bit less dramatic. They transitioned to life as working dogs on Italian farms, tackling tasks like hunting wild boar, herding livestock, and keeping watch over property. This loyal breed fulfilled these roles for centuries, but sadly, by the mid-20th century, factors like war, economic hardship, and modern farming practices pushed the breed towards extinction. Thankfully, this story has a happy ending. In the 1970s, dedicated dog enthusiasts rediscovered some surviving Cane Corsi and set out on a mission to bring the breed back. Fast forward to 1983, and the Society of Cane Corso Lovers was born in Italy, working tirelessly to revive the breed. The United States wasn’t left out for long. In the late 1980s, Michael Sottile imported the first litter of Cane Corsos, where they exploded in popularity across America. A few years later, the International Cane Corso Association was established, and by 2010, the Cane Corso officially received recognition from the American Kennel Club. Today, the Cane Corso Association of America governs the breed in the United States. The name “Cane Corso” itself gives us some into the breed’s history. “Cane” comes from the Latin word “canis,” simply meaning “dog.” “Corso” has two possible origins: one theory suggests it comes from “cohors,” translating to “bodyguard,” reflecting their protective nature. Another theory suggests it stems from the old Italian word “corsus,” meaning “sturdy” or “robust,” in a nod to their impressive build. The Italian Mastiff’s temperament is a complex blend of loyalty, protectiveness, intelligence, and independence. They can be quiet, attentive, and super serious, but that stems from their desire to be protective guardians. Much like all dogs, a Cane Corso’s behavior all depends on how they’ve been raised since puppyhood. That said, we’re talking about Cane Corsos. If you don’t take charge, they will. Protective:  Bred for centuries as guardians, Cane Corso mastiff possess a strong protective instinct towards their family and property. They are naturally alert and watchful. Loyal:  These dogs form deep bonds with their families and are fiercely loyal companions. Intelligence and Trainability: Eager to Please:  Cane Corsos are intelligent dogs who are eager to please their owners. This makes them highly trainable with the right approach. Confident and Independent:  Cane Corsos possess a confident and independent streak. Experienced dog owners who can provide consistent leadership will find them a rewarding breed. The good news is that Cane Corsos are intelligent dogs who are eager to please their human parents. This makes them highly trainable with the right approach. Positive Reinforcement:  Positive reinforcement training techniques are ideal for building a strong bond and fostering obedience. Their intelligence allows them to learn quickly, but consistency and patience are key. However, Cane Corso’s require a firm hand and are not recommended for first-time dog parents. Independent Streak:  Cane Corsos possess a confident and independent streak. They are not pushovers and require experienced dog owners who can provide consistent leadership. Reserved with Strangers:  While not inherently aggressive, Cane Corsos can be reserved with strangers. Early socialization from a young age is crucial to prevent them from becoming wary or fearful of unfamiliar people. Strong Will:  Their independent nature can sometimes translate to stubbornness. Owners should be prepared to establish themselves as the leader through consistent training and boundaries. Overall, Cane Corsos are loyal, intelligent, and protective companions who thrive in homes that can provide consistent training, ample exercise, and a strong sense of leadership. While their guardian instinct makes them excellent watchdogs, proper socialization is crucial to ensure they are well-adjusted around strangers and other animals. The short answer is that Cane Corsos are neither inherently dangerous nor aggressive. Many perceive the Cane Corso’s temperament as dangerous because of their powerful build. However, when well-trained and properly socialized, a Cane Corso will hardly pose a danger to everyone they meet. By nature, Cane Corsos have strong protective instincts. Therefore, they’re often quick to defend loved ones and property from anything they perceive as a threat. It’s in a Cane Corso’s nature to be distrustful of strangers and other pets. Unfortunately, their natural urge to defend and protect is often mistaken for innate aggression. But make no mistake, the Cane Corso bite force is an estimated 700 PSI. So when owners don’t fulfill their pet parent responsibilities to the letter, they can become aggressive if not properly trained. Early Socialization is Key:  While naturally reserved with strangers, early socialization from a young age helps them become well-adjusted around new people and other animals. While there are no federal bans on Cane Corsos in the United States, some cities and states have Breed Specific Legislation (BSL) and ordinances that restrict, regulate, or prohibit ownership of certain dog breeds, including Cane Corsos. Those states are: Alabama, Arkansas, Colorado, Idaho, Kansas, Nebraska, South Dakota, and Washington. As of December 2023, the United Kingdom’s “Dangerous Dog Act,” didn’t ban the Cane Corso, but many Corso parents feel this might be a pretext for a ban to eventually happen. Other European countries such as Denmark, Norway, France, and Malta have varying degrees of restrictions. The laws and restrictions are subject to change at any time, and this information is for general guidance and may not be fully comprehensive. Are King Cane Corsos real? Let’s get this out of the way now: There is no official designation for a King Cane Corso dog. The name is just a marketing gimmick used by breeders to inflate their prices. Established breed standards exist for Cane Corsos, outlining their size, proportions, and other characteristics. There’s no mention of a “King” classification within these standards. If you’re considering a Cane Corso puppy, it’s important to find a reputable breeder who adheres to breed standards and focuses on health and temperament. Avoid breeders who use terms like “King Cane Corso dogs” as it might not be a reliable indicator of quality. As per  Cane Corso Pets , purchasing a Cane Corso for sale from a reputable breeder typically falls within the $1,500 to $2,500 range. On the other hand, those seeking a Cane Corso puppies with top breed lines and a superior pedigree can expect to pay between upwards to $5,000 or more. Cane Corso are expensive dogs to own. If you choose the purchase, the initial upfront cost could range anywhere between $1,500 to as high as $8,000 if you’re looking for superior, championship bloodlines. Rescuing and adopting can greatly reduce this cost. If you are considering getting a Cane Corso, be sure to do your research and make sure that you are prepared for the commitment. These dogs are amazing companions, but they require a lot of care and attention. The Cane Corso’s short, sleek coat may appear low-maintenance, but consistent grooming is essential for their overall health and well-being. By following these simple grooming practices, you can help your Cane Corso maintain a healthy, shiny coat, and an overall practical grooming schedule. While Cane Corsos don’t require frequent baths, the occasional cleansing is necessary for their health. Getting your Cane Corso accustomed to grooming procedures from a young age makes the process easier and more enjoyable for both of you. Handle their paws frequently, examine their mouth and ears, and reward them for good behavior during grooming sessions. This positive foundation sets the stage for stress-free veterinary exams and handling throughout their lives. Compared to their cousin, the Neapolitan Mastiff, the Cane Corso has a lighter build. This is due to their historical roles, which went beyond guarding property. These versatile farmhands were bred to hunt wild game, keep watch over livestock, and even assist with herding duties. Imagine a Cane Corso rounding up pigs or cattle, helping to drive them to market. They don’t want to be sitting on a couch. So for an outlet for their innate instincts and mental stimulation, Corsos want to work – so give them a job! Good employment for a Corso includes herding livestock, learning tricks, practicing obedience skills, or being involved in a dog sport.  Today, they are used as therapy dogs and service dogs to help people with a variety of disabilities, including autism and post-traumatic stress disorder. Due to their susceptibility to bloat and Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus (see Cane Corso Health Issues below), special care should be taken when feeding your Corso. Always consult your veterinarian for specific feeding recommendations based on your individual Cane Corso’s needs. They can consider your dog’s age, weight, activity level, and overall health to determine the optimal feeding plan. For puppies ages 2-6 months, 3-4 meals per day with a total of 3-4 cups of high-quality puppy food specifically formulated for large breeds is recommended. As they approach their first year, you can gradually reduce the feeding frequency to 2-3 meals per day. Stick to a Schedule:  Feeding your puppy at consistent times throughout the day helps regulate their digestion and prevents them from becoming overly hungry. Measure Food:  Use measuring cups to ensure you’re feeding the correct amount of food. Choose High-Quality Food:  Invest in a high-quality puppy food specifically formulated for large breeds. These foods provide the essential nutrients your puppy needs for healthy growth and development. Avoid Free Feeding:  Free feeding (leaving food out all day) can lead to overeating and obesity. Fresh Water:  Always provide your puppy with fresh, clean water at all times. A full grown Cane Corso typically requires feeding twice a day. Younger adults (1-3 years old) may need slightly more food than older adults (4+ years old) due to higher activity levels. The same feeding tips for a puppy apply here, but with an adult Corsi, there are other factors to consider. Activity Level:  Highly active Cane Corsos will burn more calories and require more food than their less active counterparts. Body Weight and Condition:  Monitor your dog’s weight and adjust food portions as needed to maintain a healthy body condition. Their ribs should be easily felt but not visible. Food Quality:  High-quality dog food offers more concentrated nutrients, allowing you to feed a smaller amount compared to lower-quality fo How much your adult dog eats depends on his size, age, build, metabolism, and activity level. Dogs are individuals, just like people, and they don’t all need the same amount of food. It almost goes without saying that a highly active dog will need more than your average lap dog. The Corso is a serious dog breed for a person who is serious about having a dog as a companion and who can provide them with the firm, but loving guidance they need to become a well-adjusted dog and happy member of the family. However, once they are a member of the family, they are a family-only dog. Don’t expect them to buddy up with everyone they meets. Corsos have no interest in people or other animals outside his family, but those within the family will have his undivided loyalty and protection. Cane Corsos make great family dogs. When properly raised, trained, and socialized, the Corso can be loving toward and protective of children. Running and squealing may cause the Corso to associate children with prey. Keep your Corso confined when kids run outdoors and make lots of noise, especially if your children have friends over. The Corso may think it necessary to step in and protect “their” kids, which is unlikely to end well. Games of fetch or — for young children — helping to hold the leash are good ways for children to interact with a Cane Corso puppy or adult. Never allow a Corso to run loose. A solid, secure fence is a must. An electronic fence will not prevent him from leaving your property if he chooses to, and it won’t protect your neighbor’s dog or cat if he wanders into your yard. As with every breed, you should always teach children how to approach and touch dogs and supervise any interactions between dogs and young children to prevent biting or ear or tail pulling on the part of either party. Teach your child never to approach any dog while it’s eating or sleeping or to try to take the dog’s food away. The Corso may get along with other dogs or cats if raised with them, but will likely view strange animals as prey. It’s essential to be able to protect neighbors’ pets from him. This is another instance in which socialization is a must. Your Cane Corso should learn from an early age to remain calm in the presence of other dogs. If you get a second dog, either Cane Corso or a different breed, it is best to choose one of a different sex. Cane Corsos can potentially live in apartments, but it requires a significant commitment from their owner to ensure their needs are met. Ultimately, the decision depends on your lifestyle and commitment .  If you can provide the exercise, training, and mental stimulation a Cane Corso needs, apartment living can be successful. Like all breeds, Cane Corsos have potential health concerns. Responsible breeders prioritize genetic health testing to minimize these risks. The Cane Corso is predisposed to various forms of heart disease, such as dilated cardiomyopathy. This condition affects the heart’s ability to pump blood effectively throughout the body. You should also be aware of the most common Corso health problems, so that you can seek veterinary care right away. Due to their size, Corsos may develop joint problems in their front and back legs that can cause them discomfort and, eventually, severe pain later in life. If your Corso has a limited range of motion or a limp, hip and elbow dysplasia are the likely causes. A  PennHIP screening  can evaluate your Corso’s risk of hip dysplasia. Verify hip dysplasia clearances (OFA/PennHIP) from puppy’s parents. Reputable breeders prioritize genetic testing over a simple vet check to ensure healthy bloodlines. Don’t buy from breeders who lack documentation. Treatment for these issues can include pain and anti-inflammatory medications, joint supplements, physical therapy, and even surgery. However, monitoring your Corso’s weight can also go a long way as a preventative measure. Large, deep-chested dog breeds like Cane Corsos are at risk for bloat (gastric dilatation) and GDV (stomach torsion). These conditions are life-threatening and require immediate veterinary attention. Warning signs include rapid stomach enlargement, unproductive vomiting, abdominal pain, and changes in behavior. Bloat might be treated medically, but GDV requires emergency surgery. Preventative gastropexy surgery, during spaying or neutering, attaches the stomach to the body wall, stopping torsion. Cane Corso dogs are susceptible to four distinct eye conditions, mostly concerning their eyelids. Expect breeders to have up-to-date health clearances certifying that a puppy’s parents are free of eye disease. These clearances should be in the form of an eye exam by a board-certified veterinary ophthalmologist with the results registered with the  Orthopedic Foundation for Animals  (OFA). The Corso can develop allergies to food ingredients, environmental allergens such as pollen or dust mites, or both, resulting in itchy skin, ear infections, and digestive issues. When choosing a Cane Corso breeder, avoid those who lack written health testing documentation for the breed. A simple vet check isn’t enough – responsible breeders rely on genetic testing to ensure healthy bloodlines. This proactive approach minimizes the risk of inheritable health issues in your future furry companion. As stated, prioritizing ethical breeders is a must. While DogTime will always advocate for adoption or rescue first, we understand the necessity of ethical breeding practices. Some Corsos, purchased without any clear understanding of what goes into owning one, need adoption or fostering. The  Cane Corso Rescue, Inc ., or a local breed club, who can assist you in adopting a Cane Corso in need of a good and loving home.

Height: 10 inches to 12 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The Cardigan Welsh Corgi, the older of the two Corgi breeds, is a herding dog believed to have existed in Wales for over 3,000 years. Throughout history, farmers relied on these speedy, hardworking, and versatile dogs to drive cattle to market. These days, they mostly love to spend time with their families. The Cardigan Corgi is distinguished from the Pembroke Welsh Corgi by their long tail — like the sleeves of a cardigan sweater. Their medium-length coat comes in many colors and patterns, including red, brindle, blue merle, and black, usually with white markings. Known as the yard-long dog, the breed is sensible and affectionate. If you’re planning to bring a Corgi puppy home, do a bit of research on the best ways to care for raising this active, happy-go-lucky breed. When considering a Cardigan Welsh Corgi or Cardigan Welsh Corgi puppy, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase, choosing a  reputable breeder  is crucial. Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Cardigan Welsh Corgi breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs. They also conduct necessary health screenings and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Indigenous to the United States, the Carolina Dog is a rare, primitive dog breed and relatively new to domesticity. They are medium in size,  agile , and independent; they’re also known for being very clean dogs. The Carolina Dog is also sometimes called the Yellow Dog, the American Dingo, the Dixie Dingo, and the Yaller. They were feral and lived in the Southeastern United States for hundreds of years, and they’re still found in the wild in some parts of Georgia and South Carolina. Carolina Dogs are probably not going to be overly affectionate but will form close bonds with their humans. Show them strong leadership and let them know you’re in charge when  training . They may act reserved and wary of strangers, but they don’t tend to behave aggressively. These dogs are pack-oriented through and through, and they’d thrive in multi-dog and person homes, forming loving bonds with other dogs and humans alike. They have a high  prey drive , so you must watch them closely around other small animals. These  pups  love big families and big homes with yards where they can run around. Carolina Dogs are incredibly loyal to their humans and sweet and  playful with kids. When considering a Carolina Dog, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase a  Carolina Dog puppy , it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Carolina Dog breeders prioritize health and temperament, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Catahoula Bulldog is a fierce and affectionate hybrid breed resulting from the crossbreeding of the  Catahoula Leopard Dog  and the  American Bulldog . Known for their striking appearance and versatile nature, they are a capable companion for various roles. With their muscular build, intelligent demeanor, and protective instincts, the Catahoula Bulldog has gained popularity among dog owners seeking a loyal and hardworking canine companion. Physically, Catahoula Bulldogs have a robust and athletic stature, reflecting the strength and agility of both the American Bulldog and the Catahoula Leopard Dog. Their coat, often showcasing the distinctive merle pattern characteristic of Catahoula Leopard Dogs, adds to their eye-catching appearance.  Beyond their striking looks, Catahoula Bulldogs are intelligent and adaptable, excelling in tasks such as hunting, herding, and guarding. As loyal and devoted family members, they thrive with proper  training ,  socialization , and plenty of  physical activity , making them well-suited for active households seeking a devoted and capable companion. When considering a  Catahoula Bulldog puppy , it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase, it’s crucial to choose  a reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Catahoula Bulldog breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Height: 20 to 26 inches tall at the shoulder
Description: The Catahoula Leopard Dog, or Catahoula Cur, is a medium to large herding dog from Louisiana originally bred to hunt hogs. Because they were bred to work in swamps and forests, they are tough dogs that require a firm and consistent leader with time to keep them occupied. Interestingly, Catahoulas are named after the Catahoula Parish in Louisiana, where they were first bred. While these pups are very affectionate with their humans, they need a lot of exercise. Consequently, they will do best in a home with a large, fenced-in yard and experienced pet parents. Furthermore, Catahoula Leopard Dogs are also known for their unique vocalizations, including barks, howls, and yelps. In summary, If you can meet their needs, you will have a loving family member who will keep you on your toes. As with every dog, Catahoula puppies require early socialization to help them adjust to this exciting new world! When considering a Catahoula Leopard Dog, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. The Caucasian Shepherd Dog or Caucasian Ovcharka Dog is a fierce family protector originating in the Caucasus Mountain region, specifically Georgia, Azerbaijan, Armenia, and Dagestan. They’re also known as Russian Bear Dogs, Baskhan (Karachay) Pariy, or just CO, and many variants exist. This massive breed is highly territorial and won’t back down from a fight, even against bears or wolves. Caucasian Shepherd Dog temperament is intelligent, alert, protective, and calm, but their independent nature can make them difficult to train, and they are not recommended for novice owners. Their natural distrust of strangers and other animals can also lead to aggressive tendencies if an experienced trainer doesn’t keep them in check. Though they are fairly low-energy dogs, the sheer size of Caucasian Shepherds makes them poorly suited for  apartment living  and homes with small children. You may want to keep this in mind if you’re a first-time pet parent searching for a companion. While  Caucasian Shepherd puppies  may be fluffy and adorable, they take a strong hand and a lot of work. Still, with proper training and socialization, well-trained Caucasian Shepherd Dogs can make strong watchdogs, family companions, and even therapy dogs. When considering a Caucasian Shepherd Dog, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Caucasian Shepherd Dog breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Cav-a-Jack is a mixed breed dog–a cross between the  Cavalier King Charles Spaniel  and  Jack Russell Terrier  dog breeds. Energetic, friendly, and playful, these pups inherited some of the best qualities from both of their parents. You can find these mixed breed dogs in shelters and breed specific rescues, so remember to always adopt! Don’t shop if you’re looking to add a Cav-a-Jack to your home! The Cav-a-Jack is a great dog for a first time owner to consider. These pups are playful and sociable; they love to spend time around people and will make great playmates for any young children in your household. They also have something of a mischievous side! Due to the mixed breed’s smaller size, they’re adaptable to apartment living, just as long as you make sure you meet their exercise requirements. Cav-a-Jacks are known to be smart and inquisitive dogs who will benefit from interactive toys in their environment. See below for all mixed dog breed traits and facts about Cav-a-Jacks!

The Cavachon is a small-sized companion crossbreed between a  Cavalier King Charles Spaniel  with a  Bichon Frise . Combining the Cavalier’s affectionate nature with the Bichon’s playful demeanor, the Cavachons are beloved for their sweet temperament, intelligence, and irresistible appearance. Sporting a soft, fluffy coat in various colors, including white, apricot, and cream, these affectionate dogs have expressive eyes and floppy ears. The Cavachon has recently gained popularity for their suitability as a family pet and companion. Specifically, they are known for their adaptability to various living environments, including apartments and houses; Cavachons thrive on companionship and excel in roles as  therapy dogs . Lastly, their gentle disposition and friendly nature make them excellent additions to households with children and other pets. At the same time, their intelligence and trainability ensure they are quick learners which is great for first-time pet parents. Cavachons, a mix of Cavalier King Charles Spaniel and Bichon Frise, can have a wide variety of coat colors. Common variations include white or white with tan, apricot, or chestnut markings. They may also inherit the Cavalier’s classic tri-color pattern, featuring white with black and tan highlights, or the Blenheim coloring, with chestnut patches on a white coat. Some Cavachons have sable coats, blending dark and light hairs for a textured look, while others may have solid shades like tan, apricot, or light brown. Their coat can be wavy, curly, or silky, depending on the parent breed’s influence. Cavachons typically have a compact, well-proportioned body with an adorable, slightly rounded face. Their size ranges from small to medium, although size can vary based on the parents’ genetics. On average, Cavachons weigh between 15 to 20 pounds and stand about 12 to 13 inches tall at the shoulder. However, their size can vary slightly depending on the specific genetics they inherit from their Cavalier King Charles Spaniel and Bichon Frise parents. Some Cavachons may be a bit smaller or larger, but they generally remain within this size range. This modern designer breed was first intentionally bred in the late 1990s in the United States, primarily to combine the most desirable traits of both parent breeds, particularly their affectionate, gentle nature and  hypoallergenic coat . While the Cavachon is relatively new, their parent breeds have a long and storied history. The Cavalier King Charles Spaniel dates back to the 16th century in England, where they were favored by royalty, including King Charles I and King Charles II. Originally bred as lap dogs and companions for aristocrats, the Cavalier is known for its sweet temperament, loyalty, and silky, flowing coat. Their affectionate and gentle nature also made them ideal companion animals for noble families. The Bichon Frise is believed to have originated in the Mediterranean region, where they were popular among sailors. Later, they became the favored pets of European nobility, especially in Spain, France, and Italy. The Bichon Frise is known for their cheerful, playful personality and hypoallergenic, curly coat, which sheds minimally. Cavachons are known for their affectionate, friendly, and gentle personalities. They also form strong bonds with their families and love being close to their humans. They often seek attention and affection, which makes them ideal companion dogs. However, they don’t fare well left alone for long periods and may be prone to separation anxiety. While they enjoy lounging and cuddling, Cavachons also have a playful side. They like games, playtime, and short bursts of activity but are not overly high-energy, making them manageable for various living situations, including apartments. Cavachons are adaptable and can adjust to different living environments, whether in a small apartment or a larger home, as long as they receive attention, love, and moderate exercise. Early socialization and positive reinforcement training are crucial due to their intelligence and potential stubbornness.  Of course, their intelligence allows them to pick up commands quickly, so early training is key to establishing good behavior from the start. Use praise, treats, and affection to reward desired behavior. Cavachons are sensitive and respond well to positive reinforcement. Cavachons, like many companion dogs, tend to thrive on human interaction and can become anxious or bored if left alone for long periods. Generally, a Cavachon can be left alone for 4 to 6 hours at a time. However, leaving them alone for longer than this regularly can lead to separation anxiety, destructive behavior, or excessive barking. As with all dogs, it’s important to keep up your Cavachon’s regular veterinary checkups to detect any health concerns early. Your vet can help you develop a care routine that will keep your dog healthy. Cavachons have moderate grooming needs due to their soft, often wavy or curly coats, which can be prone to matting and tangling. Regular grooming helps keep their coat healthy and looking their best. Grooming a Cavachon involves maintaining their coat, ears, nails, and teeth. Their grooming needs can vary depending on whether they inherit more of the Bichon’s plush, double coat or the Cavalier King Charles Spaniel’s wavy/straight coat. Getting your Cavachon accustomed to grooming procedures from a young age makes the process easier and more enjoyable for both of you. Handle their paws frequently, examine their mouth and ears, and reward them for good behavior during grooming sessions. This positive foundation sets the stage for stress-free veterinary exams and handling throughout their lives. As with all dogs, the Cavachon’s dietary needs will change from  puppyhood  to  adulthood  and will continue to change into their  senior years . You should ask your veterinarian for recommendations about your Cavachon’s diet, as there is far too much variation among individual dogs to make a specific recommendation. Cavachons are highly compatible with families and make excellent pets for households with various dynamics. Additionally, their friendly, affectionate nature and moderate energy levels make them a great fit for families of all sizes, including those with children or other pets. The Cavachon is social and enjoys the company of other dogs, as long as they receive their fair share of attention from their owner. With proper introductions and training, the Cavachon can get along with cats and other animals. As with every breed, you should always teach children how to approach and touch dogs and supervise any interactions between dogs and young children to prevent biting or ear or tail pulling on the part of either party. Teach your child never to approach any dog while it’s eating or sleeping or to try to take the dog’s food away. The Cavachon mixed breed is predisposed to some of the same conditions that the Cavalier and Bichon parents also face. While most are generally healthy, some may be prone to a few health issues, which is why it is important to maintain good care and regular veterinary checkups. The Cavador is a mixed breed dog — a cross between the  Cavalier King Charles Spaniel  and  Labrador Retriever  dog breeds. Friendly, loving, and intelligent, these pups inherited some of the best qualities from both of their parents. Cavadors are also known as the  Cavadoor or Cavadore . You can find these mixed breed dogs in shelters and breed specific rescues, so remember to always adopt! Don’t shop if you’re looking to add one of these mixed breed pups to your home! Cavadors make excellent family pets–and also frequently work as service dogs. If you’re looking for a devoted family-focused dog with warm and playful instincts, this might well be the mixed breed for you. The Cavador does exceptionally well around kids and will happily take part in joint play sessions between humans and canines. See below for all mixed dog breed traits and facts about Cavadors!

The Cavalier King Charles Spaniel is a small-sized companion breed from the United Kingdom, renowned for their affectionate and loving personality. Standing 12 to 13 inches tall at the shoulder, these affectionate dogs are classified as toy members of the Spaniel group. Named after the British monarch who popularized the breed, they thrive on human interaction and make excellent companions for individuals and families. Their warm and gentle temperament also makes them well-suited for households with children and other pets. They are known for their friendly, patient, and loving personalities. They form strong attachments with their human companions and do not do well when left alone for long periods of time. Their adaptability is a key attribute that adds to their appeal. Whether you live in a house or apartment, Cavaliers are content as long as they receive ample love and attention. They adapt well to various living situations and lifestyles, making them suitable for many owners. Although they’re born to be a companion, the Cavalier King Charles Spaniel retains the sporty nature of their ancestors. Like other companion breeds, Cavaliers are very sensitive and require patient, gentle training. When considering a Cavalier King Charles Spaniel, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase a Cavalier King Charles Spaniel puppy, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

The Cavapoo, also known as the Cavoodle, is a small-to-medium-sized hybrid breed, a cross between a  Cavalier King Charles Spaniel  and a  Poodle . These dogs are also known by several names, including Cavadoodle and Cavapoodle. Outgoing, playful, and curious, Cavapoos inherit some of the best traits from their parent breeds. Like many other designer dog breeds, the exact origins and development timeline of the Cavapoo are not well-documented. However, they have quickly become popular in the designer dog world, particularly within the “Doodle dog” category. With their compact size, unique coloring, and hypoallergenic coat, Cavapoos often resemble teddy bears. Their sweet temperament complements their charming appearance. Known for being sociable, playful, and loving, Cavapoos make excellent family pets thanks to their compatibility with children and outgoing nature. However, without proper training, they can develop destructive habits, such as chewing furniture or shoes and rummaging through trash when left alone. Cavapoos are friendly with almost everyone and may even greet an intruder with a wagging tail. If you’re looking for a guard dog, this breed is not the best choice. Cavapoos thrive in homes where they receive plenty of attention, whether in a family setting or with an individual owner. While they can adapt to being the only pet in a household, they tend to do best in a “pack” environment. The Cavapoo’s coat is typically soft, wavy, or curly, low-shedding, and hypoallergenic, making this breed a popular choice for allergy-prone owners. The texture and curliness of their coat often depend on the specific influence of the  Poodle  parent. Cavapoos come in a wide range of colors, including solid shades like white, apricot, and black, as well as multicolored patterns like tricolor, parti-color, or merle. Some may have markings that create unique and eye-catching combinations, such as white patches on a darker base or a blend of caramel and cream. Regular grooming is essential to keep their coat in good condition and prevent matting. The size of a Cavapoo depends largely on the size of their  Poodle  parent, as Poodles come in three main varieties: Toy, Miniature, and Standard. Cavapoos are most commonly bred with Toy or Miniature Poodles, as Standard Poodles result in significantly larger dogs. They’re a popular family dog, thanks to their outgoing, playful, and affectionate temperament. That said, the Cavapoo is not a dog that tolerates being alone very much, and they could experience  separation anxiety  if not properly socialized and trained. The Cavapoo is an intelligent breed and lives for your validation and praise. This makes Cavapoos easy to train, as they are eager to please. Cavapoos can be athletic, too, making them great in agility and obedience competitions. As they can be somewhat attached to the hip, it is important to train your Cavapoo early so they know how to be alone. Without proper training, your Cavapoo could turn to destructive behaviors, like chewing furniture and shoes or rooting through the garbage, when left alone. Cavapoos tend to get along with everyone and may even greet an intruder with a wag of their tail. With that in mind, if you are looking for a guard dog, a Cavapoo isn’t a good choice. Cavapoos tend to thrive most in homes where they get loads of attention, whether with a family or an individual. The Cavapoo, also known as the Cavoodle, is a relatively modern designer dog breed that originated in the late 20th century. While the exact timeline and location of their development are not well-documented, it’s believed they were first intentionally bred in the United States or Australia during the 1990s. The aim was to combine the affectionate and gentle nature of the  Cavalier King Charles Spaniel  with the intelligence, hypoallergenic coat, and low-shedding qualities of the  Poodle . Cavapoos quickly gained popularity due to their appealing physical traits, such as their teddy bear-like appearance, and their friendly, adaptable temperaments. Over the years, Cavapoos have become a beloved member of the “Doodle” category of designer breeds, which includes other popular hybrids like Labradoodles and Goldendoodles. Despite their relatively short history, Cavapoos have firmly established themselves as one of the most sought-after designer dogs. Cavapoos are prone to obesity, so portion control and limiting treats are important. Puppies, adults, and senior Cavapoos have varying dietary needs, with puppies requiring more frequent meals for growth and seniors benefiting from lower-calorie diets to support healthy aging. Cavapoos make a wonderful choice for families due to their affectionate temperament, outgoing personalities, and propensity to form strong bonds with their humans. Their playful and gentle nature suits them as a perfect companion for children, while their small size makes them suitable for homes or apartments with limited space. Cavapoos are also known to get along with other pets, including cats and other small animals, as long as they’re properly socialized. This devoted breed thrives on human interaction, which can often mean they are happier with families where there is always a guaranteed human to keep them company. The Central Asian Shepherd Dog, also known as the Alabai, Turkmen Wolf-Hound, or Alabay, is a fierce working breed who has earned a reputation for being one of the most formidable livestock guardian dogs. Originally bred to guard flocks of sheep and goats from predators such as bears and wolves, the Central Asian Shepherd Dog is renowned for their strength, independence, and protective instincts. Additionally, the Central Asian Shepherd Dog temperament is bold, curious, loyal, independent, and adaptable. An ancient breed, the Central Asian Shepherd typically weighs between 80 to 170 pounds, with females being smaller than males. The Alabai dog’s distinctive features also include a dense double coat and a strong, muscular build. Known for their loyalty to their family, guardian dogs are typically quite reserved. Their independent nature and history as working dogs make early socialization and consistent training essential. While they may be aloof with strangers, they form strong bonds with their owners. These devoted and reliable companions pair well with those who appreciate the qualities of this ancient and dignified breed. As a note, this breed may not be suited to novice or first-time dog owners. When considering a Central Asian Shepherd Dog, it’s advisable to prioritize adopting from rescue organizations or shelters to provide a loving home to a dog in need. However, if you decide to purchase a Central Asian Shepherd Dog puppy, it’s crucial to choose a  reputable breeder . Conduct thorough research to ensure that the breeder follows ethical practices and prioritizes the well-being of their dogs. Reputable Central Asian Shepherd Dog breeders prioritize the health and temperament of their dogs, conduct necessary health screenings, and provide a nurturing environment for the puppies. This active approach ensures that you bring home a healthy and happy pup while discouraging unethical breeding practices.

Description: The Cesky Terrier, also called the Bohemian Terrier, is a Czech terrier breed that descends from the  Scottish Terrier  and  Sealyham Terrier . Originally developed in the mid-20th century to hunt vermin, the Cesky Terrier’s loving personality, moderate exercise needs, and small size make them a great companion dog. Regarding temperament, the Cesky Terrier personality is cheerful, reserved, calm, and trainable. They are also very affectionate and playful with their humans. Their small size and moderate exercise needs also make them adaptable to apartment life. They even fit in well with novice pet parents and pick up on training fairly easily. While they enjoy cuddling on the couch, they can compete in several dog sports and keep up with energetic family members. The Cheagle is a delightfully quirky cross between the  Chihuahua  and  Beagle  dog breeds. These spunky little dogs range from 10-15 inches tall and tip the scales at 15-25 pounds, keeping them well in the small-sized range. With their compact size and energetic dispositions, Cheagles make ideal city-dwelling companions for active owners. Cheagles go by several names, including Beagle Chi, Chi-Bea, Beagle Chihuahua mix, and Chibeagle. What Cheagles lack in stature, they more than make up for in a bold and charismatic temperament. From the Chihuahua side, they get a sassy, demanding demeanor that commands respect. But they balance this feistiness with the Beagle’s friendly, happy-go-lucky attitude. Cheagles thrive on constant attention and aim to be involved in all family adventures.
